item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures here do a reasonable job of preparing us for the scip, but damn, maybe at least terminate the personnel before incinerating them.

+ more crit
- more crit

Honestly, after the first paragraph of description, I was expecting to hate this. Note that we never received an answer to the natural question, "what does 'quasi-fungal' mean?" I agree with earlier commentators that this suffers from at least two key problems. One, the scip is questionable as an anomaly; its supernatural characteristics are not identified in the description and are barely alluded to in the log. In fact, to keep the "reveal" in the log intact, the description actually misleads us about what is known about the entity. Two, purely in terms of containment breach risk, this thing is Keter and its current containment procedures are woefully inadequate. It can't conceivably be reliably contained without isolating it from the ocean. Granted, that might mean research into how to isolate and contain the scip is ongoing, but the containment procedures should clarify that.

Let me add a third. The submarine log really didn't work for me. It was easy to lose track of who was doing what, and the characters' behavior was pure archetype; the executive's desire to obtain a sample, using an inadequate vessel, after having confirmed the location of the lost shipment, breaks immersion. "Oh, here's the part where the crew is compelled to do something they themselves identify as irrational by the person paying them, who insists on the vessel doing exactly what is necessary for the narrative to proceed and the monster to awaken / activate / etc."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few questions that posed themselves as I read this:

If the crew got blinded by using their sub's welder at max power, why does it say they were blinded by 'an unknown force'? That makes it sound like the Foundation has no idea how or why they got blinded.

How comes this organism hasn't spread throughout the entire ocean if it is dangerous enough to warrant this kind of containment?

Conversely, why is the oil rig even still intact? Shouldn't the Foundation try to clear it out with fungicides or burn it down or sink it back into the ocean?

While the idea in general is interesting, I think the execution, especially the end, falls a bit flat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On that note, this seems more Apollyon (or at least Keter) than Euclid. Given the amount of plankton and filter-feeding organisms in the ocean, those spores will be being gobbled down like no one's business. A passing whale gulping down a mouthful, a single baitfish swallowing them while snapping at some plankton, a copepod eating one; all will lead to a new instance.

The fungicide spray might reliably prevent it from releasing spores, but that's not mentioned in the article and it had about a week of growth before the Foundation got there, then however long it took the Foundation to figure out what to do before they started spraying the fungicide. And even then, mistakes can happen; it's going to be hard to uniformly apply the fungicide to every nook and cranny of the wreckage and the rig.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slow burn buildups like this need a satisfying ending.

This one felt far to abrupt and uninteresting. I expected more horror based on how this scp was described. It certainly has a lot more potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This definitely felt inspired by the slow burn horror movies of the 80s and 90s. The log reminded me of the first act of Alien specifically. I do wish there was more, but what’s here does a suitable job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, cholera and bubonic plague are also all of that. I can't see the Foundation containing those, personally, so I have to disagree. This is a very competently written article, and I liked reading it, but unfortunately I'm gonna have to go with a novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a highly-dangerous parasitic biological organism with a high rate of spread. I believe the Foundation's smart enough to keep this contained and in check at least until they can figure out what can be done about the whole situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I fully understood this. How is this organism anomalous? Why does it warrant Foundation containment?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It looks like the boundless night sky, stars twinkling in droves.

An ocean locale log-based horror skip! This one was surprisingly inspired by the movie Kaze no tani no Naushika (Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind). I also remembered that it's sort of post-plagiarizing the Doctor Who episode Fury from the Deep, but I didn't knowingly pull anything from it at all so that's a moot point.

Just a little something to keep you guys entertained while I work on my next 90s/alternate 80s Foundation Kojima-inspired SCP.

Thanks to: Pedagon, Rounderhouse, J Dune, Ralliston, and Jakob17 for looking over this.

I'll put the license box in a little bit; I'm still grabbing all the sources again.
